title_en: Salah in Al-Muhalla: Prayer in the Dhahiri School
content_en:
Ibn Hazm's treatment of prayer in Al-Muhalla is among the most compelling chapters of the work, demonstrating the Dhahiri school's potential to arrive at compelling positions through strict textual analysis. Some of his prayer positions align with the four Sunni schools, some match one school against the others, and some are unique to the Dhahiri approach.

For al-Fatihah in prayer, Ibn Hazm is among the strongest advocates of its obligatory recitation in every rak'ah, citing the mutawatir hadith 'There is no prayer for one who does not recite the Opening of the Book' (al-Bukhari, Muslim). He goes further than some schools in insisting that this applies even to the follower praying behind an imam — the follower must recite al-Fatihah themselves during the imam's silent moments. This position, shared with the Shafi'i school, is the most textually supported position on the question.

On the opening takbir, Ibn Hazm affirms that 'Allahu Akbar' is the established formula, based on the prophetic practice. But unlike the strict Hanafi position, he argues from a different direction: the hadith 'The key of prayer is taharah, its ihram (entry) is the takbir, and its tahallul (exit) is the salam' establishes the function of the takbir as the entry-point into the prayer's sacred state, and the specific wording is established by prophetic practice rather than by the theoretical impossibility of synonyms.

Ibn Hazm's treatment of the five prayer times reveals his methodological distinctiveness. He argues directly from the hadiths describing prayer times — particularly the hadith of Jibril — without the interpretive elaborations that distinguish one school from another. His prayer times are therefore closest to what the primary texts directly state, without the additional distinctions (such as the Hanafi late 'Asr time) that other schools develop through ijtihad.

On the question of whether missing a prayer (qada') is obligatory or merely recommended, Ibn Hazm famously argued — in a position rejected by all four schools — that a person who deliberately misses a prayer has no valid qada' for it, because the texts specify that the prayer is prescribed at its specific time, and fulfilling it outside that time is not the same act. One who misses a prayer deliberately has thereby incurred a sin without a means of erasing it through qada' — they can only seek forgiveness and perform more voluntary prayers. This extreme position, though rejected by the four schools, is based on a literal reading of the relevant texts and illustrates the Dhahiri method's potential for novel conclusions.

Ibn Hazm's treatment of Friday prayer highlights his consensus with the four schools on the obligation of Jumu'ah for free adult Muslim males in a settled community, while differing on the minimum number required. He argues from the Quranic command — 'O you who believe, when the call is made for prayer on Friday, hasten to the remembrance of Allah' (62:9) — that the obligation applies to any number of Muslims present, without specifying a numerical minimum beyond the imam and the congregation (which must be more than one person to constitute a congregation at all).
title_id: Salat dalam Al-Muhalla: Pendekatan Dhahiri terhadap Ibadah Salat
content_id:
Pembahasan Ibn Hazm tentang salat dalam Al-Muhalla termasuk di antara bab-bab yang paling menarik dalam karya ini, menunjukkan potensi mazhab Dhahiri untuk mencapai posisi-posisi yang meyakinkan melalui analisis tekstual yang ketat. Beberapa posisi salatnya selaras dengan keempat mazhab Sunni, beberapa cocok dengan satu mazhab melawan yang lain, dan beberapa bersifat unik bagi pendekatan Dhahiri.

Untuk al-Fatihah dalam salat, Ibn Hazm termasuk di antara pendukung terkuat kewajiban membacanya di setiap rakaat, dengan mengutip hadis mutawatir 'Tidak ada salat bagi orang yang tidak membaca Pembuka Kitab' (al-Bukhari, Muslim). Ia melangkah lebih jauh dari beberapa mazhab dengan menegaskan bahwa ini berlaku bahkan bagi makmum yang salat di belakang imam - makmum harus membaca al-Fatihah sendiri selama momen-momen diam imam. Posisi ini, yang dimiliki bersama dengan mazhab Syafi'i, merupakan posisi yang paling didukung secara tekstual dalam masalah ini.

Tentang takbiratul ihram, Ibn Hazm menegaskan bahwa 'Allahu Akbar' adalah lafaz yang ditetapkan, berdasarkan praktik kenabian. Namun tidak seperti posisi ketat Hanafi, ia berargumen dari arah yang berbeda: hadis 'Kunci salat adalah taharah, pembukaannya (ihram) adalah takbir, dan penutupnya (tahlil) adalah salam' menetapkan fungsi takbir sebagai titik masuk ke kondisi sakral salat, dan lafaz spesifiknya ditetapkan oleh praktik kenabian bukan oleh ketidakmungkinan teoritis sinonim.

Pembahasan Ibn Hazm tentang lima waktu salat mengungkap keunikan metodologisnya. Ia berhujah langsung dari hadis-hadis yang menggambarkan waktu-waktu salat - khususnya hadis Jibril - tanpa elaborasi interpretatif yang membedakan satu mazhab dari yang lain. Waktu-waktu salatnya oleh karenanya paling dekat dengan apa yang secara langsung dinyatakan oleh teks-teks primer, tanpa pembedaan tambahan (seperti waktu Asar akhir menurut Hanafi) yang dikembangkan mazhab-mazhab lain melalui ijtihad.

Tentang apakah mengqada' salat yang terlewat itu wajib atau sekadar dianjurkan, Ibn Hazm secara terkenal berpendapat - dalam posisi yang ditolak oleh keempat mazhab - bahwa seseorang yang dengan sengaja meninggalkan salat tidak memiliki qada' yang valid untuk salat tersebut, karena teks-teks menetapkan bahwa salat diwajibkan pada waktu spesifiknya, dan menunaikannya di luar waktu itu bukan merupakan perbuatan yang sama. Orang yang meninggalkan salat dengan sengaja telah menanggung dosa tanpa sarana untuk menghapusnya melalui qada' - mereka hanya dapat memohon ampun dan melakukan lebih banyak salat sunnah. Posisi ekstrem ini, meskipun ditolak oleh keempat mazhab, didasarkan pada pembacaan literal teks-teks yang relevan dan menggambarkan potensi metode Dhahiri untuk menghasilkan kesimpulan-kesimpulan baru.

Pembahasan Ibn Hazm tentang salat Jumat menyoroti konsensusnya dengan keempat mazhab tentang kewajiban Jumu'ah bagi laki-laki Muslim merdeka dewasa yang tinggal di komunitas menetap, sambil berbeda tentang jumlah minimum yang diperlukan. Ia berhujah dari perintah Alquran - 'Wahai orang-orang yang beriman, apabila diseru untuk menunaikan salat pada hari Jumat, bersegeralah mengingat Allah' (62:9) - bahwa kewajiban berlaku untuk jumlah Muslim yang hadir berapa pun, tanpa menetapkan minimum numerik selain imam dan jemaah (yang harus lebih dari satu orang untuk membentuk jemaah).
content_ar: Ibn Hazm's treatment of الصلاة in Al-Muhalla is among the most compelling الأبواب of the work, demonstrating the Dhahiri school's potential to arrive at compelling positions through strict textual analysis. Some of his الصلاة positions align with the four Sunni schools, some match one school against the others, and some are unique to the Dhahiri approach. For al-Fatihah in الصلاة, Ibn Hazm is among the strongest advocates of its obligatory التلاوة in every rak'ah, citing the mutawatir الحديث 'There is no الصلاة for one who does not recite the Opening of the الكتاب' (البخاري, مسلم). He goes further than some schools in insisting that this applies even to the follower praying behind an الإمام — the follower must recite al-Fatihah themselves خلال the الإمام's silent moments. This position, shared with the الشافعي school, is the most textually supported position on the question. On the opening التكبير, Ibn Hazm affirms that 'الله أكبر' is the established formula, based on the prophetic practice. But unlike the strict الحنفي position, he argues from a different direction: the الحديث 'The الرئيسي of الصلاة is الطهارة, its ihram (entry) is the التكبير, and its tahallul (exit) is the salam' establishes the function of the التكبير as the entry-point into the الصلاة's sacred state, and the specific wording is established by prophetic practice rather than by the theoretical impossibility of synonyms. Ibn Hazm's treatment of the five الصلاة times reveals his methodological distinctiveness. He argues directly from the hadiths describing الصلاة times — particularly the الحديث of جبريل ﷺ — without the interpretive elaborations that distinguish one school from another. His الصلاة times are لذلك closest to what the الأساسي texts directly state, without the additional distinctions (مثل the الحنفي late 'Asr time) that other schools develop through الاجتهاد. On the question of whether missing a الصلاة (القضاء') is obligatory or merely recommended, Ibn Hazm famously argued — in a position rejected by all four schools — that a person who deliberately misses a الصلاة has no valid القضاء' for it, لأن the texts specify that the الصلاة is prescribed at its specific time, and fulfilling it outside that time is not the same act. One who misses a الصلاة deliberately has thereby incurred a الذنب without a means of erasing it through القضاء' — they can only seek المغفرة and perform more voluntary الصلوات. This extreme position, though rejected by the four schools, is based on a literal reading of the relevant texts and illustrates the Dhahiri method's potential for novel conclusions. Ibn Hazm's treatment of Friday الصلاة highlights his consensus with the four schools on the الالتزام of الجمعة for free adult مسلم males in a settled الجماعة, بينما differing on the minimum number required. He argues from the القرآني command — 'O you who believe, عندما the call is made for الصلاة on Friday, hasten to the الذكر of الله' (62
